column7: <html> element must have a lang attribute
column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Open the Browser Console.
3. Run aXe Scan.
4. Look for issue "<html> element must have a lang attribute" as it's displayed on aXe results section and select "Inspect" button to be taken to the block of code where the issue is located.
column9:
HTML document does not have a lang attribute.

If the language of a webpage is not specified, the screen reader will assume it is the default language set by the user. This becomes an issue for users who speak multiple languages and access website in more than one language.
column10: The language is specified on every single webpage.
column18:
Provide lang attribute with valid value "en" to the <html> tag. 

Refer to: 
https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20-TECHS/H57.html

column7: Document must have one main landmark
column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Open the Browser Console.
3. Run aXe scan.
4. Look for issue "Document must have one main landmark" as it's displayed on aXe results section and select "Inspect" button to be taken to the block of code where the issue is located.
column9:
There is no main landmark.

Document must have main landmark as it identifies the primary content of the page, otherwise screen reader users will not understand the layout of the page.
column10: When a page contains nested document and/or application roles, each document or application role may have one main landmark.
column18:
Provide a <main> landmark or you can also use role="main" to mark-up the principal content of the page.

Refer to:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/main
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/Main_role

column7: All page content must be contained by landmarks
column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Use key combination "insert + f7" in NVDA to open the elements list.
3. Verify the landmarks in the element list.
column9:
The HTML document does not contain header, main, and footer landmarks.

Landmarks helps user to understand the structure of the document and to identify piece of content.
column10:
All web pages should have, at least, the following landmarks:
- Header
- Main
- Footer
column18:
Use <header> tag as a container of the banner region, <main> tag as a container of main region and <footer> tag as a container of the content info region.

You can also use aria roles for each landmark:
- Header landmark: role=“banner”,
- Main landmark: role=“main”,
- Footer landmark: role=“contentinfo”,
- Navigation landmark: role=“navigation”,
- Search: role=“search”

Finally, ensure that <header>, <main>, <footer> should be a direct child of <body> tag.

Refer to:
https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/page-structure/regions/

column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Notice that the page title is not defined in the source code.
column9:
Page title is not provided for the web page, as <title> tag is empty in the source code.

This might confuse the user since there is no correlation between the title and the content being displayed on the web page. The title is very useful for user to navigate through different browser tabs.
column10:
Web pages must have titles that describe their purpose.

Example: Page title: Current Promotion GNB | West Shore Homes
column18:
Every HTML document must have a <title> tag in the <head> section.

The <title> element should be used to identify the content of an HTML document and it must be descriptive.

column7: Frames must have a title attribute
column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Open the Browser Console.
3. Run aXe scan.
4. Look for issue "Frames must have a title attribute" as it's displayed on aXe results section and select "Inspect" button to be taken to the block of code where the issue is located.
column9:
The screen reader does not announce the iframe title for the offer time countdown frame.

Screen reader users have the option to pull up a list of titles for all frames on a page. Adding descriptive, unique titles allows users to quickly find the frame they need. If titles are not present, navigating through frames quickly becomes difficult and confusing.
column10:
Every frame has a unique title.

For example: Frame title: Offer time countdown
column18:
1. Use the title attribute to define an accessible name for an iframe element.
2. Use the aria-label attribute to define an accessible name for an iframe element.
3. Use the aria-labelledby attribute to define an accessible name for an iframe element.
4. Accessible names should be short and describe the contents of the iframe element to help users decide whether to navigate to the iframe or not.
5. For iframes with no visible content, explicitly identify the iframe as having no information for the user (e.g. title="No content").

Refer to:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/ARIA_Techniques/Using_the_aria-label_attribute

column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Navigate through the webpage using arrow keys.
3. Notice the screen reader navigation sequence.
column9:
The navigation order provided to the entire webpage is not meaningful.

Note: It is impossible to access the application with the screen reader as the reading order does not match with the presentation order in every section.

Meaningful sequence requires that content presented on the page must be meaningful during navigation to all users. Users navigate content from left to right & assistive technology users such as screen reader users depend on the accessibility tree & DOM structure to let them know how the content is presented on the page.
column10:
Correct and meaningful navigation should be present for the web page.

The screen reader should read the content in the below mentioned sequence using arrow keys:
1. Header section
2. Main section
3. Footer section

Same pattern should be followed in every section present in the different regions .
column18:
For both navigation systems, using the TAB key or the arrow keys, the screen reader follows the arrangement in the document object model (DOM) and not the order in which the elements are displayed on the screen.

The screen reader should read the content in the below mentioned sequence using arrow keys:
1. Header section
2. Main section
3. Footer section
Same pattern should be followed in every section present in the different regions.

Refer to:
https://www.essentialaccessibility.com/blog/css-and-accessibility

column8:
1. Go to the URL
2. Browse through the page until you find the text "Whether you're upgrading your bath,...".
3. Open Colour Contrast Analyser and click on any pixel from the text in the foreground section using the eyedropper tool.
4. Click on any pixel in the background section using the eyedropper tool.
5. Measure the contrast ratio between the text and its irregular background color.
column9: The color contrast ratio of the  text "Whether you're upgrading your bath,..." is insufficient due to its irregular background.
column10: The color contrast ratio of the text should be of at least 4.5:1.
column18:
To achieve the minimum contrast ratio between text and an irregular background you can use one of the following techniques:
 
1. Use a background color for the text to pass the minimum contrast ratio, this is achieved by adding a span tag and a class with a background color attribute.
2. Add a <div> tag with a background-color property to pass the contrast ratio and give it a transparency.
3. Add a border to the text which passes the minimum contrast ratio.
4. Add a background gradient that has enough contrast with the text to the <div> container to pass the minimum ratio and place the text over the darkest part of the gradient.

Refer to:
https://www.essentialaccessibility.com/blog/content-over-images-how-does-this-uxui-trend-impact-accessibility/

column7: ID attribute value must be unique
column8:

column9:
More than one instance of an active ID is found.

Duplicate IDs found: lp-pom-button-45

The value assigned to active ID attributes on focusable elements must be unique to prevent the second instance from being overlooked by assistive technology.
column10: No repeated IDs are found.
column18:
Avoid having two active IDs of a different version (web mobile or desktop version) for the same element at the same time. 
In case the mobile responsive view is displaying, then the only ID that should be active is the ID of the corresponding mobile version. 
Make sure that each focusable element on the page with an active ID has a unique value.

column8:
1. Go to the URL
2. Browse through the page until you find the heading text "Affordable".
3. Open Colour Contrast Analyser and click on any pixel from the text in the foreground section using the eyedropper tool.
4. Click on any pixel in the background section using the eyedropper tool.
5. Measure the contrast ratio between the text and its irregular background color.
column9: The color contrast ratio of the heading text "Affordable" is insufficient due to its irregular background.
column10: The color contrast ratio of the text should be of at least 4.5:1.
column18:
To achieve the minimum contrast ratio between text and an irregular background you can use one of the following techniques:
 
1. Use a background color for the text to pass the minimum contrast ratio, this is achieved by adding a span tag and a class with a background color attribute.
2. Add a <div> tag with a background-color property to pass the contrast ratio and give it a transparency.
3. Add a border to the text which passes the minimum contrast ratio.
4. Add a background gradient that has enough contrast with the text to the <div> container to pass the minimum ratio and place the text over the darkest part of the gradient.

Refer to:
https://www.essentialaccessibility.com/blog/content-over-images-how-does-this-uxui-trend-impact-accessibility/

column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Navigate through the page using the keyboard until you reach the same page link "Transform Your Bath".
3. Notice that focus order is not the same as the one being displayed on the screen after selecting the same page link "Transform Your Bath".
column9:
After selecting same page link "Transform Your Bath", the screen reader focus does not automatically reach the update content "Start Designing Your New Shower". 

It is important to provide a logical navigation sequence for keyboard-only user with motor disabilities or non-visual users that can't use the mouse to navigate the page in order for them to fully understand the content.
column10: After selecting same page link "Transform Your Bath", the screen reader focus should  automatically reach the update content "Start Designing Your New Shower".
column16:
All Products GBA: After selecting the same page links "Remodel My Home, Schedule A Free Quote", focus does not move to the updated form content, 

Door Replacement GBA: After selecting the same page links "Remodel My Door, See For Yourself, Remodel My Entry Door, Remodel My Patio Door, and get started", focus does not move to the updated form content, 

Door Replacement GNB: After selecting the same page links "Remodel My Door, See For Yourself, Remodel My Entry Door, Remodel My Patio Door, Remodel My Door and get started", focus does not move to the updated form content, 

After selecting same page link "Remodel My Bathroom", the screen reader focus does not automatically reach the update form content.
column18:
Pass the #id of the related section to each same page link.

Use the JS function "element.focus()" to manage the screen reader focus.

Refer to: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLElement/focus

column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Navigate through the page using the keyboard until you reach the buttons under heading "SOME OF OUR FAVORITES".
3. When the button is triggered, notice that a modal is being displayed and focus does not move directly to it.
column9:
The modal window don't have a logical navigation sequence using only the keyboard.

It is important to provide a logical navigation sequence using only the keyboard for people with motor disabilities or non-visual users that can't use the mouse to navigate the page in order for them to fully understand the content.
column10:
When a modal is displayed, the following should be verified: 
1. Whether focus is on the first element which can be focused on or on the entire modal.
2. Whether focus is trapped within the modal or not and whether it's cyclic (from the last element to the first and from the first element to the last).
3. Whether it's closed by pressing the ESC key.
4. Whether focus goes back to the element which opened the modal once the modal is closed.
column16:
All Products GB: After selecting the button "see more details', focus does not move to the modal window, 

Door Replacement GBA: After selecting the button "see more details', focus does not move to the modal window, 

Door Replacement GNB: After selecting the button "see more details', focus does not move to the modal window.
column18:
Trap focus within the modal using the JS focus() function and make sure to order the elements of the HTML in a logical way, so that the default tab order follows the sequence.

Refer to:
https://uxdesign.cc/how-to-trap-focus-inside-modal-to-make-it-ada-compliant-6a50f9a70700
https://hiddedevries.nl/en/blog/2017-01-29-using-javascript-to-trap-focus-in-an-element

For screen reader add the following:
Also, add a role="dialog", an aria-modal="true" and an aria-labelledby="ID_TITLE_MODAL" to the <div> that wrap the modal.

Refer to :
https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices/examples/dialog-modal/dialog.html

column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Navigate through the website until reaching the buttons under heading "SOME OF OUR FAVORITES".
3. Notice that the screen reader does not announce the button's purpose.
column9:
The screen reader announces the buttons as "unlabelled" present under heading "SOME OF OUR FAVORITES".

This could potentially confuse the user because they are not aware of the action that is going to be performed.
column10:
The screen reader announces the action that is going to be performed when the button is triggered.

Example for Labels according to functional images: Main door, Side Window, Hand Wash in washroom, etc
column18:
Use an aria-label or aria-labelledby attribute to provide a proper label for the button.

Refer to:
https://developers.google.com/web/fundamentals/accessibility/semantics-aria/aria-labels-and-relationships

column6:
Windows/ Chrome / NVDA
macOS/ Safari/ VoiceOver
iPhone/ Safari/ VoiceOver
column8:
1. Go to the URL.
2. Navigate through the website using keyboard navigation or tab navigation until reaching the link "(866) 885-0316".
3. Notice the purpose of this link is not being correctly communicated to screen reader users.
column9:
The link "(866) 885-0316" is not descriptive or accurate, neither is it providing accurate information about where it is redirecting the user to.
 
This might be causing some difficulties for all users to navigate the website—but particularly for those with cognitive, language, and learning disabilities.
column10: The adjacent text "Call Us Today: (866) 885-0316" provide full context about where the user is going to be redirected to.
column18:
Three possible actions
a. Add more descriptive text within the <a> tag.
b. Use an aria-label or aria-labelledby attribute to provide proper context to each link.
c. Use an aria-describedby attribute to add more information about the link.

Refer to:
https://developers.google.com/web/fundamentals/accessibility/semantics-aria/aria-labels-and-relationships
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/ARIA_Techniques/Using_the_aria-describedby_attribute

2. Navigate through the page until you reach the quoted text "The entry door looks fantastic..., I am so pleased with our new door and storm door...".
3. Notice that the screen reader announces it as plain text.
column9:
The screen reader reads the quoted text "The entry door looks fantastic..., I am so pleased with our new door and storm door..." as plain text.

When blockquotes are not properly marked up, the navigation with a screen reader turns confusing or inaccurate.
column10: The screen reader reads the text "The entry door looks fantastic..., I am so pleased with our new door and storm door..." as a quotation.
column16: Door Replacement GNB
column18:
1. Wrap the quote in a <figure> to make the CSS styling easier.
2. Use the <blockquote> tag at the beginning of the quote.
3. Add <cite> tag to wrap the source of the quote.

Refer to:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/blockquote
